Appearances:
James Jacobs, Esq., and Valerie Wagner, Esq., of GCA Law Partners LLP, via Zoom videoconference, (650) 428-3900, for Plaintiff and Cross-Defendant, Donald Norman.

Zachary J. Alinder, Esq., and Ellen V. Leonida, Esq., of SIDEMAN & BANCROFT LLP, in person, (415) 392-1960, for Defendants and Cross-Complainants.

Not argued/contested. The Matter is discussed. The court confirms with counsel that there are no objections to the untimely filing and service of the subject motion. Accordingly, the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: The unopposed motion to seal is granted. The court has reviewed the material to be sealed and the papers in support of this motion and finds that the material to be sealed contains confidential financial information and other customer information. The redactions are narrowly tailored. The court finds that (1) There exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the record; (2) The overriding interest supports sealing the record; (3) A substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; (4) The proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and (5) No less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit to Dept. 301 a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Default And Quash Service Of Process For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction
Off Calendar Aug-22-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances.

RULING: There being no appearance or objection, the tentative ruling is adopted on DEFENDANT AMIR TAAKI's Motion To Set Aside Default And Quash Service Of Process For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction, as follows:

Defendant Taaki's motion to vacate the default entered on July 10, 2018 based upon Code of Civ. Proc., Sec. 473(b) is denied. The motion is untimely as the default was entered more than 6 months ago. "The six-month limit is mandatory; a court has no authority to grant relief under section 473, subdivision (b), unless an application is made within the six-month period." (Arumbula v. Union Carbide Corp. (2005) 128 Cal.App.4th 333, 340.) The Court has no authority to grant relief from the six-month limit, whether based on mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect or an attorney's affidavit of fault. (Id. at 345; accord, Austin v. Los Angeles Unified School Dist. (2016) 244 Cal.App.4th 918, 928 ["The six-month time limitation is jurisdictional; the court has no power to grant relief under section 473 once the time has lapsed."].)

[Refer to word minutes for complete ruling.]

The Court signed the proposed order submitted by email.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Not reported.

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Aug-08-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS PATRICK STRATEMAN AND JAMIE STRATEMAN'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication.

APPEARANCES: Attys. PETER ROLDAN, ESQ. and JOHNNY J. YEH, ESQ. of Emergent Law (Tel: 415-894-9284) appearing for plaintiff Donald Norman;
Attys. ZACHARY J. ALDINER, ESQ. and ELLEN P. LIU, ESQ. of Sideman & Bancroft LLP (Tel: 415-392-1960) appearing for defendants Patrick Strateman and Jamie Strateman.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Patrick Strateman's motion for summary judgment or alternatively summary adjudication is denied in its entirety.

Defendants fail to show that the statute of limitations bars plaintiff's claims. The parties agree that Delaware law applies as this case involves the internal affairs of a Delaware LLC. In In re Verisign, Inc. (2007) 531 F.Supp.2d 1173, 1215, the court explains:

Delaware applies a three-year statute of limitations to claims of tort, contract, and breach of fiduciary duty. [citation] The three-year limitation period is also applied to equitable claims by analogy. [citations] "The statute of limitations begins to run at the time that the cause of action accrues, which is generally when there has been a harmful act by a defendant. This is true even if the plaintiff is unaware of the cause of action or the harm."

The parties further agree that the statute of limitations is measured from June 25, 2016, when plaintiff filed his federal complaint. Defendants fail to produce evidence showing that plaintiff's claims accrued by June of 2013. Defendants' evidence shows that by September of 2012, plaintiff had some doubts about Mr. Strateman and wondered whether he was acting in the best interests of the LLC. The deposition testimony that defendant relies upon is fairly vague, however, and does not show that plaintiff was aware that defendant took the LLC's bitcoins or caused other concrete damage to the LLC. (Defendants' UMF 1-17.) Plaintiff testified that during that time period, he did not believe that defendant would steal from the LLC. (Norman Depo., 248:18-22.) Defendants fail to maintain their summary judgment burden of production.

Mr. Strateman's motion for summary adjudication on the breach of fiduciary duty cause of action is denied. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether he was the managing member of the LLC. (See Defendants' UMF 19, 20.) Mr. Strateman admits that by September 2012, he was the only employee of the LLC and he was singlehandedly operating it. (See Defendants' UMF 137, 139, 140.) There is also a disputed issue of fact whether he breached his duty to the LLC and caused damage. For example, there is a dispute whether Mr. Strateman froze out plaintiff and whether he was allowed to take a salary. (Strateman Dec., Paras. 7-8; Norman Dec., Paras. 3, 10, 18.)

The motion for summary adjudication is denied on the aiding and abetting claims. Ms. Strateman fails to show that she did not aid and abet her son's breach of fiduciary duty and conversion. (See Defendants' UMF 359-371; 706-718.) In addition, plaintiff presents evidence that Ms. Strateman knew that her son was drawing a salary, failed to tell plaintiff, and represented to plaintiff that her son was doing everything in his power to preserve the LLC's value. (Norman Dec., Para. 21; J. Strateman Depo., 51:21-52:19; 119:11-120:3; 131:12-132:1; 135:16-137:1.)

Ms. Strateman's motion for summary adjudication is granted on the unjust enrichment claim. Plaintiff did not oppose this request and there is no evidence that Ms. Strateman (as opposed to her son) was unjustly enriched.

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano, CSR#8600, e-mail: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-24-2018 Overruled
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Peter Roldan, Esq. 415-894-9284 x103 for Plaintiff.
Ellen Liu, Esq. 415-392-1960 of Sideman Bancroft for Defendant PATRICK STRATEMAN, JAMIE STRATEMAN.

RULING - A R G U E D. Defendant argues equitable estoppel. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT PATRICK STRATEMAN, JAMIE STRATEMAN's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED DERIVATIVE COMPLAINT.

Defendants Patrick Strateman and Jamie Strateman's demurrer to all six causes of action in the plaintiff first amended derivative complaint filed by plaintiff Donald Norman is overruled as to all six causes of action. Mr. Norman allegations that Mr. Strateman unilaterally ceased operations of the company and took its assets for his personal benefit suffice to make Mr. Strateman an interested director per Delaware law. (Rales v. Blasband (Del. 1993) 634 A.2d 927, 936 ("A director is considered interested where he or she will receive a personal financial benefit from a transaction that is not equally shared by the stockholders.")). The allegations that Mr. Taaki was subject to the influence of Mr. Strateman out of fear of not receiving his share of the company's assets which Mr. Strateman allegedly usurped as well as Mr. Taaki's alleged liability for breach of fiduciary duty suffice to demonstrate that as of the filing of the complaint he was not an independent director. Mr. Norman has sufficiently alleged that defendants are estopped from relying on the three year statute of limitations based the alleged misleading statements that Mr. Strateman was working to preserve the value of the company for all three shareholders, promised to disburse the company assets, and in 2015 asked Mr. Norman to prepare a notarized document accepting a specific amount of bitcoin in satisfaction of his entitlement to the company's assets. Mr. Norman has also alleged sufficient facts to support his claims for breach of fiduciary duty, unjust enrichment, aiding and abetting and accounting. While the law is unsettled whether bitcoin should be treated as money or as property for the purposes of various legal issues, for the purpose of a claim for conversion bitcoin is fairly treated as property and thus Mr. Norman has pled sufficient facts to support a claim of conversion.
(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold E. Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, mtorreano@sftc.org, Reported. (D302/HK)
